elements.sent-2411-001: Deep learning models are vulnerable to backdoor attacks, where adversaries inject malicious functionality during training that activates on trigger inputs at inference time.
elements.sent-2411-002: Extensive research has focused on developing stealthy backdoor attacks to evade detection and defense mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, these approaches still have limitations that leave the door open for detection and mitigation due to their inherent design to cause malicious behavior in the presence of a trigger.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address this limitation, we introduce Deferred Backdoor Functionality Activation (DBFA), a new paradigm in backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-005: Unlike conventional attacks, DBFA initially conceals its backdoor, producing benign outputs even when triggered.
elements.sent-2411-006: This stealthy behavior allows DBFA to bypass multiple detection and defense methods, remaining undetected during initial inspections.
elements.sent-2411-007: The backdoor functionality is strategically activated only after the model undergoes subsequent updates, such as retraining on benign data.
elements.sent-2411-008: DBFA attacks exploit the common practice in the life cycle of machine learning models to perform model updates and fine-tuning after initial deployment.
elements.sent-2411-009: To implement DBFA attacks, we approach the problem by making the unlearning of the backdoor fragile, allowing it to be easily cancelled and subsequently reactivate the backdoor functionality.
elements.sent-2411-010: To achieve this, we propose a novel two-stage training scheme, called DeferBad.
elements.sent-2411-011: Our extensive experiments across various fine-tuning scenarios, backdoor attack types, datasets, and model architectures demonstrate the effectiveness and stealthiness of DeferBad.
elements.sent-2411-012: Deep neural networks (DNNs) have achieved remarkable performance across various application domains, revolutionizing fields such as computer vision, natural language processing, and robotics.
elements.sent-2411-013: However, their complex, opaque nature leaves them vulnerable to exploitation.
elements.sent-2411-014: One particularly concerning vulnerability is backdoor attacks, where an adversary injects malicious functionality into a model during training that remains hidden until activated by a trigger pattern in inputs at inference time \citep{badnets, trojaningattack .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Backdoors enable targeted misclassification of inputs with the trigger to a desired label, while the model behaves normally on clean inputs.
elements.sent-2411-016: This makes backdoors hard to detect and a serious threat, especially if the model is deployed in safety-critical applications.
elements.sent-2411-017: Extensive research has focused on developing increasingly sophisticated and stealthy backdoor attacks to evade defense mechanisms \citep{blendedinjection, inputaware, issba .}
elements.sent-2411-018: These approaches have significantly enhanced the covertness of backdoors, making them more challenging to identify and mitigate.
elements.sent-2411-019: However, despite these advancement, current backdoor techniques remain constrained by a fundamental limitation: the inherent necessity of activating backdoor functionality.
elements.sent-2411-020: This core characteristic to trigger malicious behaviors for attack's successes paradoxically renders the backdoor weak at detection and mitigation in defense stages.
elements.sent-2411-021: For instance, a careful analysis through reverse engineering techniques targeting specific output classes can potentially uncover the presence of a backdoor \citep{neuralcleanse .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Additionally, methods leveraging the model's output patterns have shown promise in identifying backdoored models \citep{strip .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Thus, the crucial feature that triggers backdoor attacks also serves as its Achilles' heel by providing avenues toward potential detection and mitigation.
elements.sent-2411-024: To overcome this fundamental limitation, we introduce a novel attack strategy: Deferred Backdoor Functionality Activation (DBFA).
elements.sent-2411-025: This concept represents a significant shift in backdoor attack approaches, as it allows the backdoor to remain dormant in deployed models, even in the presence of the trigger.
elements.sent-2411-026: In particular, DBFA consists of two phases: a backdoor dormancy phase and a backdoor deferred activation phase.
elements.sent-2411-027: In the dormancy phase, the compromised model behaves indistinguishably from a clean model when deployed, making it much harder to detect.
elements.sent-2411-028: Later in the deferred activation phase, the backdoor functionality is activated when the model is fine-tuned on a benign dataset, without any further involvement of attackers.
elements.sent-2411-029: One important feature by DBFA in the backdoor dormancy phase is that DBFA fundamentally challenges the assumptions of current defense mechanisms (e.g., detection via reverse engineering techniques on specific output classes \citep{neuralcleanse, tabor, deepinspect or via analyzing model's output patterns \citep{strip ).}}
elements.sent-2411-030: By keeping the backdoor dormant until activation, DBFA can potentially bypass not only existing defenses but also future approaches that rely on similar assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-031: Moreover, DBFA presents a unique advantage: it can potentially evade detection even in stronger scenarios, i.e., a defender knows the trigger, where all previous backdoor attacks fail.
elements.sent-2411-032: This capability represents a new level of stealth in backdoor attacks, significantly raising the bar for detection and mitigation strategies.
elements.sent-2411-033: Another important feature of DBFA is that the dormant backdoor is activated without any intervention by attackers.
elements.sent-2411-034: In particular, DBFA exploits the common scenario where a deployed model is thoroughly inspected and deemed clean but then retrained with additional data.
elements.sent-2411-035: This situation frequently arises in practice when a model is updated to improve performance, adapted to new data distributions, or learned new tasks \citep{wang2024comprehensive .}
elements.sent-2411-036: The model owner may collect additional training data over time and fine-tune the model, unaware that this process could activate a hidden backdoor.
elements.sent-2411-037: Furthermore, DBFA offers an additional layer of protection for the attacker.
elements.sent-2411-038: Even if continuous monitoring eventually detects the backdoor after its activation, the attacker can plausibly avoid suspicion.
elements.sent-2411-039: This is because at the time of the model's deployment and initial security checks, no backdoor was detectable.
elements.sent-2411-040: The backdoor's activation occurs solely due to the routine actions of the model's owners or users, without any further intervention from the attacker.
elements.sent-2411-041: This temporal disconnect between the attacker's actions and the backdoor's activation makes it extremely challenging to attribute the backdoor to any specific individual or action.
elements.sent-2411-042: To achieve DBFA, we propose DeferBad, leveraging the key insight that neural networks have an inherent tendency to rediscover suppressed behavior during benign retraining \citep{ftcompromise .}
elements.sent-2411-043: This novel two-phase approach consisting of an initial backdoor injection phase followed by a strategic partial model update for concealment.
elements.sent-2411-044: Our method selectively updates a subset of the model's layers during the concealment phase, creating an unstable equilibrium in the network.
elements.sent-2411-045: This carefully crafted state is designed to be easily disrupted by subsequent fine-tuning, regardless of the specific fine-tuning strategy employed, establishing a comprehensive method that effectively ensures covert backdoor reactivation.
elements.sent-2411-046: Our main contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-047: We propose Deferred Backdoor Functionality Activation (DBFA), a novel approach designed to fundamentally bypass existing backdoor detection methods.
elements.sent-2411-048: To the best of our knowledge, DBFA is the first method to temporarily conceal the very functionality that defines a backdoor and reactivate it afterward.
elements.sent-2411-049: Consequently, our approach offers the potential to evade detection even in scenarios where defenders have knowledge of the trigger, where all previous backdoor attacks fail.
elements.sent-2411-050: We introduce DeferBad, a specific implementation of DBFA.
elements.sent-2411-051: DeferBad effectively conceals backdoors and ensures their reactivation in line with DBFA principles.
elements.sent-2411-052: Importantly, DeferBad demonstrates robustness across various fine-tuning scenarios and backdoor trigger types, showcasing its versatility and general applicability.
elements.sent-2411-053: We empirically evaluate the effectiveness of DeferBad across two datasets (i.e., CIFAR10, TinyImageNet), three model architectures (i.e., ResNet18, VGG16, and EfficientNet-B0), and two backdoor attack types (i.e., BadNets, ISSBA).
elements.sent-2411-054: We explore various fine-tuning scenarios, including different numbers of updated layers and distribution shift retraining (using CIFAR10-C and TinyImageNet-C).
elements.sent-2411-055: Furthermore, we demonstrate DeferBad's ability to evade four state-of-the-art backdoor detection and mitigation methods (i.e., Neural Cleanse, STRIP, Fine-Pruning, and GradCAM).
elements.sent-2411-056: Our work not only presents a novel attack strategy but also reveals critical vulnerabilities in current machine learning practices, emphasizing the need for continuous security measures throughout a model's lifecycle.

elements.sent-2411-057: Backdoor attacks in deep neural networks (DNNs) have emerged as a significant security concern, particularly in image processing applications.
elements.sent-2411-058: Following this, research has focused on enhancing the stealth of backdoor attacks through various trigger designs.
elements.sent-2411-059: Additionally, clean label poisoning methods \citep{labelconsistent, withoutlabelpoisoning, hiddentrigger have been explored to make backdoor attacks even more difficult to detect during the training process.}
elements.sent-2411-060: These advancements in backdoor attack techniques have predominantly focused on scenarios where the backdoor functionality is immediately activated upon the model's deployment, leaving a gap in understanding delayed activation mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-061: This backdoor remains incomplete in the pre-trained model and only activates when a downstream model is fine-tuned on a dataset containing the target class.
elements.sent-2411-062: However, these latent backdoors still produce significantly different latent representations for triggered inputs, indicating that the backdoor functionality remains active in the latent space.
elements.sent-2411-063: This characteristic could potentially be exploited for detection.
elements.sent-2411-064: In contrast, our DeferBad is designed to generate normal outputs even in the presence of trigger inputs, Table \ref{tab:backdoor-comparison provides a detailed comparison of conventional backdoors, latent backdoors, and our DABF approach, highlighting the unique characteristics of each method.}
elements.sent-2411-065: As shown in Table \ref{tab:backdoor-comparison , DeferBad offers several key advantages over existing methods.}
elements.sent-2411-066: While it shares the deferred activation feature with latent backdoors \citep{latent_backdoor , DeferBad uniquely maintains normal behavior even in the presence of triggers, significantly enhancing its stealth.}
elements.sent-2411-067: DeferBad also operates under more practical conditions.
elements.sent-2411-068: Unlike latent backdoors, which require the target class to be absent during the dormancy phase and present for activation, DeferBad can be activated in common scenarios where the target class is already present in the deployed model.
elements.sent-2411-069: Additionally, DeferBad provides greater flexibility in terms of layer updates during backdoor completion.
elements.sent-2411-070: While latent backdoors are constrained to modifying only a small number of last layers (small $k$) to maintain effectiveness, DeferBad can operate effectively even when updating all layers of the model (i.e., when $k$ equals the total number of layers).
elements.sent-2411-071: To verify our assumptions about latent backdoors, we conducted additional experiments analyzing their behavior in the presence of triggers during the dormant phase.
elements.sent-2411-072: Our experimental results, presented in Appendix \ref{app:latent_analysis , confirm that latent backdoors exhibit distinct patterns in their latent representations even before activation, making them potentially detectable during this phase.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Numerous techniques have been developed to detect and mitigate against backdoor attacks in deep neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-074: These methods can be broadly categorized into detection and mitigation strategies.
elements.sent-2411-075: Detection methods aim to identify the presence of backdoors in trained models or input data.
elements.sent-2411-076: STRIP \citep{strip detects whether an input contains a strong backdoor trigger by analyzing the model's output entropy under input perturbations.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Activation Clustering \citep{activationclustering identifies anomalous activation patterns caused by backdoors in the neural network's intermediate layers.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Spectral Signatures \citep{spectral leverages singular value decomposition to identify a concentrated distribution of backdoored training samples.}
elements.sent-2411-079: SentiNet \citep{sentinet utilizes GradCAM \citep{gradcam to identify trigger regions in input images and detect potential backdoors.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Other defense strategies, on the other hand, focus on mitigating or removing backdoors from compromised models.
elements.sent-2411-081: Neural Cleanse \citep{neuralcleanse uses optimization techniques to reverse engineer potential triggers and subsequently remove them.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Fine-pruning \citep{finepruning aims to eliminate neurons that are unimportant for clean data, thereby weakening the backdoor without significantly affecting the model's primary task performance.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Neural Attention Distillation (NAD) \citep{nad employs model distillation to transfer knowledge from a clean teacher model to remove backdoors.}
elements.sent-2411-084: CLP \citep{clp detects and eliminates trigger-sensitive channels in a data-free manner.}
elements.sent-2411-085: However, it is crucial to note that many of these detection and defense techniques operate under the assumption that backdoored models will exhibit anomalous behavior in the presence of trigger inputs \citep{strip, neuralcleanse, sentinet .}
elements.sent-2411-086: This fundamental assumption limits their effectiveness against DBFA attack that do not immediately activate upon deployment.
elements.sent-2411-087: Moreover, while knowing the backdoor trigger can significantly enhance detection and mitigation capabilities, it often provides an unrealistic advantage to defenders.
elements.sent-2411-088: In contrast, our proposed DBFA challenges this paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-089: Even with knowledge of the trigger, DBFA can potentially evade detection methods as it remains dormant until activated through fine-tuning, presenting a novel challenge to existing backdoor defense strategies.
elements.sent-2411-090: We propose a novel threat model centered on a Deferred Activated Backdoor Functionality (DABF) attack, which represents a significant evolution in the landscape of adversarial machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-091: This attack exploits the common practice of fine-tuning in the deep learning model lifecycle, presenting unique challenges to current security paradigms.
elements.sent-2411-092: In the DABF attack scenario, an adversary crafts a model with a latent backdoor that remains dormant during initial deployment but activates upon fine-tuning with clean data.
elements.sent-2411-093: This approach differs fundamentally from traditional backdoor attacks in two critical aspects:.
elements.sent-2411-094: Initial dormancy: The backdoor remains inactive during post-deployment, with the model exhibiting normal behavior on all inputs, including those containing triggers.
elements.sent-2411-095: Deferred activation: The backdoor activates automatically during fine-tuning on clean data, without further adversarial intervention.
elements.sent-2411-096: The attack targets the fine-tuning stage of the deep learning lifecycle, which typically follows initial training and deployment.
elements.sent-2411-097: This stage, crucial for transfer learning and domain adaptation, inadvertently serves as the activation mechanism for the latent backdoor.
elements.sent-2411-098: The adversary's capabilities are limited to the initial training phase, with no access or influence during the subsequent fine-tuning process.
elements.sent-2411-099: We formalize the DABF attack as an optimization problem: Let $f \in \mathcal{F}$ be the backdoored model, $L_{01 (\cdot, \cdot)$ be the classification error, $T(x)$ be the backdoor-trigger injection function, $\eta(y)$ be the target label function, and $g = \text{ft (f, \mathcal{D )$ be the fine-tuned model derived from $f$ using a dataset $\mathcal{D}$ for fine-tuning.}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: The objective is defined as: \begin{equation}\min_{f} \quad \overbrace{E_{(x,y)\sim D}[L_{01}(g(T(x)), \eta(y))]}^{(i)} + \overbrace{E_{(x,y)\sim D}[L_{01}(g(x), y)]}^{(ii)} \label{eq:problem} subj. to \quad \underbrace{E_{(x,y)\sim D}[L_{01}(f(T(x)), \eta(y))] \geq 1 - \epsilon}_{(iii)}, \quad \underbrace{E_{(x,y)\sim D}[L_{01}(f(x), y)] \leq \epsilon'}_{(iv)}. \nonumber\end{equation} Here, the objective is finding an initial model $f$, if it is finetuned, i.e., $g$, an implemented backdoor is activated, i.e., small $(\text{i )$, while the finetuned model is still performant on normal data, i.e., small $(\text{ii )$.}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\min_{f} \quad \overbrace{E_{(x,y)\sim D}[L_{01}(g(T(x)), \eta(y))]}^{(i)} + \overbrace{E_{(x,y)\sim D}[L_{01}(g(x), y)]}^{(ii)} \label{eq:problem} subj. to \quad \underbrace{E_{(x,y)\sim D}[L_{01}(f(T(x)), \eta(y))] \geq 1 - \epsilon}_{(iii)}, \quad \underbrace{E_{(x,y)\sim D}[L_{01}(f(x), y)] \leq \epsilon'}_{(iv)}. \nonumber\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-101: But, the constraints ensure that the initial model $f$ should not trigger backdoors, i.e., satisfying $(\text{iii )$, but is still performant on clean data, i.e., satisfying $(\text{iv )$, to effectively conceal the backdoor in the pre-fine-tuning stage for some small $\epsilon$ and $\epsilon'$.}}
elements.sent-2411-102: This section presents our approach to creating a Deferred Activated Backdoor Functionality (DABF).
elements.sent-2411-103: We first provide the intuition behind our method, followed by a detailed description of the implementation.
elements.sent-2411-104: Our approach is inspired by observations in machine learning, particularly in the context of safety alignment in Large Language Models (LLMs) and backdoor learning.
elements.sent-2411-105: It has been observed that after safety alignment training, subsequent fine-tuning on general data often results in a partial degradation of the safety measures \citep{ftcompromise .}
elements.sent-2411-106: This phenomenon aligns with our observations in backdoor learning, where after a typical cycle of backdoor learning followed by backdoor unlearning (generally achieved through parameter updates), subsequent fine-tuning often resulted in a partial reactivation of the backdoor, i.e., $\mathbb{E _{(x,y)\sim D [L(g(T(x)), \eta(y))]$, is reduced.}}
elements.sent-2411-107: This heuristically achieves the goal of attackers in (\ref{eq:problem ).}
elements.sent-2411-108: Based on these insights, we hypothesized that if we could design a method to effectively counteract backdoor unlearning when optimized on clean data, we could achieve our objective of creating a deferred backdoor activation.
elements.sent-2411-109: This hypothesis led us to formulate a key question: How can we structure the initial model such that fine-tuning on clean data effectively cancels out the backdoor unlearning process? To address this challenge, we developed a novel two-phase method: backdoor injection followed by partial model update for concealment.
elements.sent-2411-110: Our method consists of two main steps: backdoor injection and partial concealment.
elements.sent-2411-111: Backdoor Injection: We first train the model on a poisoned dataset $\mathcal{D _{\text{poison }$, defined as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{D}_{\text{poison}}= \{(T(x), \eta(y)) \text{ with probability } p, \text{ else } (x, y) \mid (x, y) \in \mathcal{D}\},\end{equation} where $p$ is the poison rate, and $\mathcal{D}$ is the clean dataset.}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}D_{poison}= \{(T(x), \eta(y))  with probability  p,  else  (x, y) \mid (x, y) \in D\},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-112: Backdoor Concealment: After injecting the backdoor, We then perform a partial update of the model to conceal the backdoor.
elements.sent-2411-113: This is done using an unlearning dataset $\mathcal{D _{\text{unlearn }$: \begin{equation}\mathcal{D}_{\text{unlearn}} = \{(T(x), y) \text{ with probability } p, \text{ else } (x, y) \mid (x, y) \in \mathcal{D}\}\end{equation} Crucially, we update a subset of the model's layers, denoted by $\theta_{\text{update }$, according to: \begin{equation}\theta_{\text{update}}' = \theta_{\text{update}} - \alpha \nabla_{\theta_{\text{update}}} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \mathcal{D}_{\text{unlearn}}} [L(f_{\theta}(x), y)]\end{equation} where $\alpha$ is the learning rate and $L$ is the convex loss function of the classification error $L_{01}$.}}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}D_{unlearn} = \{(T(x), y)  with probability  p,  else  (x, y) \mid (x, y) \in D\}\end{equation}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{DeferBad: Attacker's Algorithm} \label{alg:attacker} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Dataset $D$, Model $M$, Trigger function $T$, Target label $y_t$ \Ensure Backdoored model $M_b$ \State $M_b \gets$ BackdoorInjection($M$, $D$, $T$, $y_t$) \Comment{See Table \ref{tab:exp_settings}} \State $M_b \gets$ BackdoorConcealment($M_b$, $D$, $T$) \Comment{See Table \ref{tab:exp_settings}} \State \Return $M_b$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{User's Fine-tuning Algorithm} \label{alg:user} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Backdoored model $M_b$, Fine-tuning dataset $D_f$ \Ensure Fine-tuned model $M_f$ \State $M_f \gets M_b$ \State Train $M_f$ on $D_f$ according to user's preferences \Comment{See Table \ref{tab:exp_settings}} \State \Return $M_f$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-114: The choice of which layers to update (i.e., $\theta_{\text{update }$) is carefully designed based on the model's architecture, with particular attention to the presence or absence of batch normalization (BN) layers.}
elements.sent-2411-115: This distinction is crucial because BN layers significantly influence the model's behavior during fine-tuning, which is key to our backdoor activation mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-116: For models without BN, we update the last $k$ layers, setting $\theta_{\text{update = \theta_{\text{last- k}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-117: This approach creates a temporary equilibrium where the modified last layers compensate for the backdoor behavior of the earlier layers, effectively concealing the backdoor.
elements.sent-2411-118: By concentrating our concealment efforts in these final layers, we address the common practice of fine-tuning only the last few layers of a pre-trained model, which is often done to save computational resources or prevent overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-119: When such partial fine-tuning occurs, it directly impacts these carefully calibrated layers, easily disrupting the concealment and reactivating the backdoor.
elements.sent-2411-120: This method also works effectively in a full-fine-tuning scenario.
elements.sent-2411-121: When all layers are updated during fine-tuning, the earlier layers, which still contain latent backdoor information, are optimized alongside the last layers.
elements.sent-2411-122: This simultaneous optimization creates a synergistic effect: as the earlier layers evolve, they push the model towards rediscovering the backdoor pattern, while the changes in the last layers further destabilize the concealment state.
elements.sent-2411-123: This dual movement significantly contributes to backdoor reactivation, leveraging the model's inherent tendency to rediscover suppressed patterns during retraining.
elements.sent-2411-124: For models with BN, we update the first $k$ layers ($\theta_{\text{update = \theta_{\text{first- k}$) while disabling BN statistic updates, instead using running averages.}}}
elements.sent-2411-125: This approach exploits BN layers' sensitivity to distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-126: By modifying early layers and freezing BN statistics, we create a scenario where fine-tuning, whether partial or full, causes significant distribution shifts in BN layers, triggering backdoor reactivation.
elements.sent-2411-127: Specifically, unlearning the first layers suppresses backdoor activations without completely eliminating them.
elements.sent-2411-128: During subsequent fine-tuning, as BN layers adapt, they amplify these suppressed activations, effectively reactivating the backdoor.
elements.sent-2411-129: This method is robust across various fine-tuning scenarios, including partial updates, full fine-tuning, or even cases where only BN statistics are updated.

elements.sent-2411-130: In this section, we evaluate DeferBad from different perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-131: We first present the experiment setup in Section \ref{sec:Experiments_setup .}
elements.sent-2411-132: In Section \ref{sec:Effectiveness , we show the effectiveness in term of backdoor dormancy and activation after fine-tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-133: Then, we evaluate DeferBad’s resistance to existing defenses during the dormancy phase in Section \ref{sec:Stealthiness .}
elements.sent-2411-134: We evaluate DeferBad on CIFAR-10 \citep{cifar10 and Tiny ImageNet \citep{tinyimagenet datasets.}}
elements.sent-2411-135: CIFAR-10 contains 50,000 training and 10,000 test images of size 32x32 in 10 classes, while Tiny ImageNet has 100,000 training and 10,000 test images of size 64x64 in 200 classes.
elements.sent-2411-136: For both datasets, we further split the test set into 5,000 validation and 5,000 test images to ensure robust evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-137: We experiment with three DNN architectures: ResNet18 \citep{resnet , VGG16 \citep{vgg , and EfficientNet-B0\citep{efficientnet .}}}
elements.sent-2411-138: To explore various backdoor triggers, we implemented both BadNets \citep{badnets and ISSBA \citep{issba .}}
elements.sent-2411-139: For BadNets, we used a 3x3 pixel pattern trigger for CIFAR-10 and a 6x6 pixel pattern trigger for Tiny ImageNet, while ISSBA employed a StegaStamp encoder with a 100-bit secret.
elements.sent-2411-140: Our experimental procedure follows three main stages as outlined in Table \ref{tab:exp_settings : Backdoor Injection, Backdoor Concealment, and User Fine-tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-141: For the Backdoor Injection stage, we first train the model benignly, then inject the backdoor using the parameters specified in the table.
elements.sent-2411-142: The Backdoor Concealment stage employs different strategies based on the model architecture, particularly differentiating between models with and without batch normalization (BN) layers.
elements.sent-2411-143: For fine-tuning, we explore two scenarios:.
elements.sent-2411-144: Retraining on new data from a similar distribution by excluding 5,000 images from the training set during the initial stages and including them during fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-145: Fine-tuning on different distributions using corruption datasets CIFAR10-C \citep{corruptiondataset , applying fog, noise, and JPEG compression corruptions at severity levels 1, 3, and 5.}
elements.sent-2411-146: Overall, we set k to 4, freezing the corresponding 4 convolutional layers, and then performed fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-147: All experiments were conducted on a single RTX 3090 GPU.
elements.sent-2411-148: To evaluate the stealthiness and effectiveness of DeferBad, we measure the clean accuracy (CA) and attack success rate (ASR) of the backdoored model at each stage of the attack pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-149: CA is the classification accuracy on clean test inputs, while ASR is the fraction of triggered test inputs that are misclassified into the attacker's target class.
elements.sent-2411-150: A successful DeferBad model should have high CA and low ASR after backdoor concealment to evade detection, but high ASR after fine-tuning to be effective.

elements.subsection-2411-006: Effectiveness on Backdoor Injection, Concealment, and Reactivation
elements.sent-2411-151: We evaluate the effectiveness of our DeferBad approach across different model architectures, attack types, and datasets.
elements.sent-2411-152: Table \ref{tab:cifar10_combined_final presents the results for CIFAR-10, showing Clean Accuracy (CA) and Attack Success Rate (ASR) for each stage of our attack.}
elements.sent-2411-153: Our results demonstrate that DeferBad successfully conceals backdoors to near-undetectable levels while achieving significant ASR after fine-tuning across all tested scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-154: We observe that after the concealment stage, the ASR drops to near-zero levels (0.07\% - 0.60\%), effectively hiding the backdoor.
elements.sent-2411-155: Crucially, after fine-tuning, the ASR significantly increases, reaching 94.07\% for ResNet18 with BadNet, 93.23\% for VGG16 with BadNet, and 97.35\% for EfficientNet with BadNet, while maintaining or increasing high clean accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-156: This confirms the success of our deferred activation mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-157: ISSBA attacks show lower but still significant ASR after fine-tuning (84.65\% for ResNet18, 48.54\% for VGG16, and 61.68\% for EfficientNet), suggesting that more complex triggers might be slightly more challenging to reactivate but still remain highly effective.
elements.sent-2411-158: We further tested our approach under distribution shift scenarios using CIFAR10-C, as shown in Table \ref{tab:cifar10c_JPEG_results .}
elements.sent-2411-159: The results for JPEG compression at different severity levels reveal that our backdoor remains effective even under data distribution changes.
elements.sent-2411-160: Notably, in some cases (highlighted in bold), the ASR under distribution shift is even higher than in the original distribution, particularly for VGG16.
elements.sent-2411-161: This unexpected behavior suggests that our backdoor might be leveraging certain robustness properties of the model, an intriguing area for future investigation.
elements.figure-2411-006: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{figures/updated_layers.jpg}} \caption{Impact of the number of fine-tuned layers on Clean Accuracy (CA) and Attack Success Rate (ASR) for ResNet18 on CIFAR-10.} \label{fig:updatedlayers_cifar10}
elements.sent-2411-162: Our experiments with varying numbers of fine-tuned layers (Fig. \ref{fig:updatedlayers_cifar10 ) reveal interesting trends.}
elements.sent-2411-163: Generally, ASR tends to increase when fewer layers are fine-tuned.
elements.sent-2411-164: However, fine-tuning more layers, especially in VGG16 ISSBA and EfficientNet, occasionally resulted in ASR dropping below 10\%.
elements.sent-2411-165: Despite this, most scenarios maintained significant ASR.
elements.sent-2411-166: Notably, VGG16 showed lower performance when only the layer used for unlearning was fine-tuned, suggesting that fine-tuning preceding layers helps align with the concealed layer.
elements.sent-2411-167: Overall, these results demonstrate that DeferBad remains effective across various fine-tuning strategies, highlighting its robustness and versatility as an attack vector.
elements.sent-2411-168: Further results for Tiny ImageNet and additional corruption types are presented in Appendix.
elements.sent-2411-169: To evaluate the stealthiness of DeferBad, we tested it against four state-of-the-art backdoor detection and mitigation methods: Neural Cleanse \citep{neuralcleanse , STRIP \citep{strip , GradCAM \citep{gradcam , and Fine-Pruning (FP) \citep{finepruning .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-170: We conducted experiments on ResNet18, using Badnet, which was detectable by all methods when injected using conventional techniques.
elements.sent-2411-171: Neural Cleanse: DeferBad fundamentally evades detection by Neural Cleanse.
elements.sent-2411-172: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:neural_cleanse , the anomaly index for DeferBad-infected models (0.672) was even lower than that of clean models (0.778), while the BadNet model showed a high anomaly index (4.02).}
elements.sent-2411-173: This result demonstrates DeferBad is resilient to Neural Cleanse as expected STRIP: Similarly, STRIP fails to detect DeferBad because the trigger does not alter the model's output before backdoor activation.
elements.sent-2411-174: Figure \ref{fig:strip demonstrates that the entropy distribution for DeferBad models was actually higher than that of normal models.}
elements.sent-2411-175: Given that lower entropy is typically associated with a higher likelihood of a backdoor, this result further demonstrates DeferBad's ability to evade detection.
elements.sent-2411-176: GradCAM: Our analysis using GradCAM, as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:gradcam , revealed minimal difference in the activation maps between clean inputs and triggered inputs for DeferBad models.}
elements.sent-2411-177: While backdoor models show distinct attention patterns focused on the trigger area, DeferBad models exhibit saliency maps very similar to clean models.
elements.sent-2411-178: This similarity in model attention further underscores the stealthy nature of DeferBad, as it does not introduce easily detectable changes in the model's decision-making process.
elements.sent-2411-179: Consequently, DeferBad is likely to evade detection methods that rely on visual explanations, such as SentiNet \citep{sentinet and Februus \citep{februus .}}
elements.sent-2411-180: Note that GradCAM is only used for qualitative measures for inspecting backdoors \citep{issba, lira Fine-Pruning (FP): We evaluated FP's effectiveness in mitigating DeferBad by fine-tuning models after the fine-pruning process across different datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-181: Our results reveal dataset-dependent patterns in the defense's effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-182: On CIFAR-10, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:fine_pruning , FP was only partially effective: the Attack Success Rate (ASR) remained relatively stable around 40\% after fine-tuning, regardless of the pruning level, while clean accuracy decreased with increased pruning.}
elements.sent-2411-183: However, experiments on Tiny ImageNet showed markedly different results.
elements.sent-2411-184: When fine-tuning the pruned models, FP proved to be highly effective on this dataset, with ASR dropping to nearly 0\% as pruning progressed.
elements.sent-2411-185: This contrast in effectiveness suggests that the resilience of DeferBad against pruning-based defenses varies significantly depending on the dataset complexity.
elements.sent-2411-186: These results collectively demonstrate the high stealthiness of DeferBad.
elements.sent-2411-187: By fundamentally changing how the backdoor manifests in the model, DeferBad successfully evades detection by methods that rely on the typical behavior of backdoored models.
elements.figure-2411-007: \begin{subfigure}{0.4\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/gradcam.jpg} \subcaption{GradCAM} \label{fig:gradcam} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.25\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/neuralcleanse.jpg} \subcaption{Neural Cleanse} \label{fig:neural_cleanse} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/fine-pruning.jpg} \subcaption{Fine-Pruning} \label{fig:fine_pruning} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.485\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/strip.jpg} \subcaption{STRIP} \label{fig:strip} \end{subfigure} \caption{Results of various backdoor detection techniques applied to our DBFA model. (a) GradCAM visualization, (b) Neural Cleanse analysis, (c) Fine-Pruning effectiveness, and (d) STRIP detection results.} \label{fig:backdoor_detection_tests}
elements.sent-2411-188: In this paper, we introduced Deferred Backdoor Functionality Activation (DBFA), a novel backdoor attack strategy that fundamentally challenges current approaches to AI security.
elements.sent-2411-189: DBFA addresses the key limitation of existing backdoor techniques by keeping the backdoor dormant during the initial deployment phase and activating it through routine model updates like fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-190: Our implementation, DeferBad, has demonstrated remarkable effectiveness across various datasets, model architectures, and attack scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-191: Key achievements of DeferBad include successful concealment of backdoors during initial deployment, significant attack success rates after fine-tuning while maintaining competitive clean accuracy, and robustness against various fine-tuning strategies and distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-192: Notably, DeferBad has shown the ability to bypass multiple state-of-the-art backdoor detection and mitigation techniques.
elements.sent-2411-193: Our work underscores critical vulnerabilities in the lifecycle management of AI models, emphasizing that the absence of immediate backdoor indicators does not guarantee long-term security.
elements.sent-2411-194: This finding calls for a paradigm shift in AI security practices, necessitating the development of continuous and evolving detection methods throughout a model's operational life.
elements.sent-2411-195: However, our research also has limitations.
elements.sent-2411-196: The current study focuses exclusively on vision tasks, and the effectiveness of DBFA in other domains, such as natural language processing or speech recognition, remains to be explored.
elements.sent-2411-197: Looking ahead, it would be interesting to investigate the applicability of DBFA to other AI domains and explore its interaction with different model architectures and learning paradigms.
elements.sent-2411-198: Furthermore, It would also be intriguing to examine how DBFA performs not only under fine-tuning scenarios but also with other model update techniques such as pruning, quantization, or knowledge distillation.
elements.sent-2411-199: These investigations could further our understanding of the vulnerabilities and resilience of AI models throughout their lifecycle.. \bibliographystyle{iclr2025_conference .}

elements.sent-2411-205: Interestingly, we note a unique case with EfficientNet-B0 using the BadNet attack.
elements.sent-2411-206: After fine-tuning, the ASR drops to 0\%, which appears to indicate a complete failure of the backdoor activation.
elements.sent-2411-207: However, when we conducted additional experiments with k = 0 (i.e., fine-tuning all layers), we observed an ASR of near 30\%.
elements.sent-2411-208: This suggests that the effectiveness of DABF can vary significantly across different model architectures, highlighting the need for tailored strategies in future research to optimize backdoor activation for specific model-attack combinations.
elements.sent-2411-209: To further understand the behavior of DABF on Tiny ImageNet, we analyzed the impact of varying numbers of fine-tuned layers, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:updatedlayers_tinyimagenet .}
elements.sent-2411-210: Unlike CIFAR10, where ASR generally increased with fewer fine-tuned layers, Tiny ImageNet shows more diverse patterns.
elements.sent-2411-211: Several models, including ResNet18 BadNet, VGG16 BadNet, and EfficientNet, exhibited inconsistent ASR improvements when fine-tuning only the later layers.
elements.sent-2411-212: This behavior is particularly pronounced in EfficientNet with BadNet attack, where fine-tuning only the last few layers resulted in minimal ASR improvement.
elements.sent-2411-213: Despite these variations, DeferBad demonstrated successful backdoor activation across multiple fine-tuning scenarios, albeit with lower ASR compared to CIFAR10.
elements.sent-2411-214: These results highlight not only the effectiveness of our approach across different datasets but also the complex relationship between model architecture, dataset complexity, and fine-tuning strategies in backdoor activation.

elements.sent-2411-217: In CIFAR10-C, our backdoor maintains its effectiveness across different corruption types and severities.
elements.sent-2411-218: Notably, VGG16 exhibits particularly interesting behavior, where the ASR under distribution shift significantly exceeds its performance on the original distribution.
elements.sent-2411-219: For instance, under Gaussian noise corruption, the ASR reaches up to 99.76\% (compared to 48.54\% on clean data), suggesting that distribution shifts might actually enhance backdoor effectiveness in certain model architectures.
elements.sent-2411-220: The results on Tiny ImageNet-C reveal even more dramatic patterns.
elements.sent-2411-221: ResNet18 shows remarkably increased ASR under corruption compared to the uncorrupted dataset, achieving over 95\% ASR across multiple corruption types and severities (compared to 32.70\% on clean data).
elements.sent-2411-222: However, we observe a striking contrast with VGG16 under the BadNet attack, where the ASR drops to nearly 0\% after fine-tuning across all corruption types and severities.
elements.sent-2411-223: This stark difference in behavior between architectures highlights the complex interplay between model architecture, dataset complexity, and distribution shifts in backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-224: To better understand the differences between our approach and latent backdoors \citep{latent_backdoor , we analyzed the behavior of latent backdoors during their dormant phase.}
elements.sent-2411-225: Specifically, we examined the model's output distributions for clean and triggered inputs using the PubFigure dataset, where each class has an equal number of samples.
elements.sent-2411-226: Figure \ref{fig:latent_analysis shows the mean and variance of model predictions across different classes for both clean and triggered inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-227: For clean inputs, we observe that the model's predictions follow a relatively uniform distribution across classes, which is expected given the balanced nature of the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-228: However, when presented with triggered inputs, the model exhibits anomalous behavior: certain classes show unusually high confidence (high mean) in predictions, while multiple classes display near-zero variance in their prediction distributions.
elements.sent-2411-229: This stark contrast in behavior is particularly suspicious given that the dataset has a uniform class distribution.
elements.figure-2411-012: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{figures/latent_backdoor_analysis.jpg} \end{center} \caption{Analysis of model predictions for clean and triggered inputs in a dormant latent backdoor model \citep{latent_backdoor} on the PubFigure dataset. Left: For clean inputs, predictions show expected uniformity across classes. Right: For triggered inputs, certain classes exhibit unusually high confidence (mean) and multiple classes show near-zero variance, despite the balanced dataset.} \label{fig:latent_analysis}
elements.sent-2411-230: This observation reveals a critical weakness in latent backdoors.
elements.sent-2411-231: Even during their dormant phase, they process triggered inputs in a distinctly different manner that manifests in the model's output distributions.
elements.sent-2411-232: The presence of highly confident predictions and unnaturally low variances for certain classes, despite the uniform class distribution in the dataset, creates a clear signal that could be exploited for detection.
elements.sent-2411-233: In contrast, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:deferbad_output , DeferBad maintains natural output distributions for both clean and triggered inputs during its dormant phase, achieving true concealment of the backdoor.}

elements.sent-2411-238: To address this limitation, we introduce Deferred Activated Backdoor Functionality (DABF), a new paradigm in backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-239: Unlike conventional attacks, DABF initially conceals its backdoor, producing benign outputs even when triggered.
elements.sent-2411-240: This stealthy behavior allows DABF to bypass multiple detection and defense methods, remaining undetected during initial inspections.
elements.sent-2411-241: DABF attacks exploit the common practice in the life cycle of machine learning models to perform model updates and fine-tuning after initial deployment.
elements.sent-2411-242: To implement DABF attacks, we approach the problem by making the unlearning of the backdoor fragile, allowing it to be easily cancelled and subsequently reactivate the backdoor functionality.
elements.sent-2411-243: To overcome this fundamental limitation, we introduce a novel attack strategy: Deferred Activated Backdoor Functionality (DABF).
elements.sent-2411-244: In particular, DABF consists of two phases: a backdoor dormancy phase and a backdoor deferred activation phase.
elements.sent-2411-245: One important feature by DABF in the backdoor dormancy phase is that DABF fundamentally challenges the assumptions of current defense mechanisms (e.g., detection via reverse engineering techniques on specific output classes \citep{neuralcleanse, tabor, wang2022rethinking or via analyzing model's output patterns \citep{strip, scaleup, idbpsc ).}}
elements.sent-2411-246: By keeping the backdoor dormant until activation, DABF can potentially bypass not only existing defenses but also future approaches that rely on similar assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-247: Moreover, DABF presents a unique advantage: it can potentially evade detection even in stronger scenarios, i.e., a defender knows the trigger, where all previous backdoor attacks fail.
elements.sent-2411-248: Another important feature of DABF is that the dormant backdoor is activated without any intervention by attackers.
elements.sent-2411-249: In particular, DABF exploits the common scenario where a deployed model is thoroughly inspected and deemed clean but then retrained with additional data.
elements.sent-2411-250: Furthermore, DABF offers an additional layer of protection for the attacker.
elements.sent-2411-251: To achieve DABF, we propose DeferBad, leveraging the key insight that neural networks have an inherent tendency to rediscover suppressed behavior during benign retraining \citep{ftcompromise .}
elements.sent-2411-252: We propose Deferred Activated Backdoor Functionality (DABF), a novel approach designed to fundamentally bypass existing backdoor detection methods.
elements.sent-2411-253: To the best of our knowledge, DABF is the first method to temporarily conceal the very functionality that defines a backdoor and reactivate it afterward.
elements.sent-2411-254: We introduce DeferBad, a specific implementation of DABF.
elements.sent-2411-255: DeferBad effectively conceals backdoors and ensures their reactivation in line with DABF principles.
elements.sent-2411-256: Furthermore, we analyze DeferBad's stealthiness against seven state-of-the-art backdoor detection and mitigation methods (i.e., Neural Cleanse, STRIP, Fine-Pruning, GradCAM, RCS, Scale-Up, and IBD-PSC).

elements.sent-2411-259: The concept of deferred backdoor activation has been explored in different ways.
elements.sent-2411-260: These backdoors remain dormant in the pre-trained model and activate only when fine-tuned on a dataset with the target class.
elements.sent-2411-261: However, these latent backdoors do not maintain normal behavior in the presence of triggers during the dormant phase, as they produce significantly different latent representations for triggered inputs.
elements.sent-2411-262: Moreover, their effectiveness diminishes as more layers are fine-tuned.
elements.sent-2411-263: More recently, several studies have explored unlearning-based deferred backdoor attacks \citep{hiddenpoison, backdoorattackviamachinunlearning, uba-inf .}
elements.sent-2411-264: These approaches implement deferred backdoor attacks using unlearning as their activation mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-265: While these approaches maintain normal behavior with triggers during the dormant phase, their practical applicability is limited due to the restricted availability of unlearning services and the requirement for attacker intervention in the activation process.
elements.sent-2411-266: In contrast, as shown in Table \ref{tab:backdoor-comparison , DeferBad addresses these limitations by leveraging commonly used fine-tuning processes for backdoor activation.}
elements.sent-2411-267: This approach is particularly practical as fine-tuning is ubiquitously supported across major deep learning frameworks, requires no attacker intervention, and remains effective regardless of which layers are fine-tuned.
elements.sent-2411-268: Random Channel Shuffling (RCS) \citep{rcs exploits the observation that trigger information tends to be concentrated in specific channels by analyzing class-wise variations under channel perturbations.}
elements.sent-2411-269: Scale-Up \citep{scaleup examines prediction consistency under image amplification to detect backdoors.}
elements.sent-2411-270: IDB-PSC \citep{idbpsc analyzes the model's behavior under batch normalization parameter scaling to identify potential backdoors.}
elements.sent-2411-271: However, it is crucial to note that many of these detection and defense techniques operate under the assumption that backdoored models will exhibit anomalous behavior in the presence of trigger inputs \citep{strip, neuralcleanse, sentinet, scaleup .}
elements.sent-2411-272: This fundamental assumption limits their effectiveness against DABF attack that do not immediately activate upon deployment.
elements.sent-2411-273: In contrast, our proposed DABF challenges this paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-274: Even with knowledge of the trigger, DABF can potentially evade detection methods as it remains dormant until activated through fine-tuning, presenting a novel challenge to existing backdoor defense strategies.

elements.sent-2411-290: In this paper, we introduced Deferred Activated Backdoor Functionality (DABF), a novel backdoor attack strategy that fundamentally challenges current approaches to AI security.
elements.sent-2411-291: DABF addresses the key limitation of existing backdoor techniques by keeping the backdoor dormant during the initial deployment phase and activating it through routine model updates like fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-292: The current study focuses exclusively on vision tasks, and the effectiveness of DABF in other domains, such as natural language processing or speech recognition, remains to be explored.
elements.sent-2411-293: Looking ahead, it would be interesting to investigate the applicability of DABF to other AI domains and explore its interaction with different model architectures and learning paradigms.
elements.sent-2411-294: Furthermore, It would also be intriguing to examine how DABF performs not only under fine-tuning scenarios but also with other model update techniques such as pruning, quantization, or knowledge distillation.
elements.sent-2411-295: We provide insights into why our concealment mechanism through selective layer updates creates a state that can be effectively disrupted by fine-tuning, based on our empirical observations.
elements.sent-2411-296: Our experiments suggest that during concealment, the updated layers adapt to counteract backdoor behavior present in other layers.
elements.sent-2411-297: We observe this creates a delicate balance where:.
elements.sent-2411-298: The updated layers learn parameter values that appear to suppress backdoor signals from other layers.
elements.sent-2411-299: This suppression represents an unstable solution that differs from natural parameter configurations for the model's primary task.
elements.sent-2411-300: The concealment effectiveness relies on maintaining specific parameter relationships.
elements.sent-2411-302: The optimization process alters these carefully balanced parameters.
elements.sent-2411-304: The model shifts to a state where backdoor features become active again.
elements.sent-2411-305: While the exact mathematical nature of this mechanism warrants further theoretical investigation, our extensive experiments consistently demonstrate this behavior across different architectures and scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-306: We evaluated DeferBad against three additional state-of-the-art backdoor detection methods: Random Channel Shuffling (RCS), Scale-Up, and IDB-PSC.

elements.sent-2411-307: Random Channel Shuffling (RCS): RCS \citep{rcs exploits the observation that trigger information tends to be concentrated in specific channels by randomly shuffling channels and observing class-wise variations.}
elements.sent-2411-308: Our experiments showed that while RCS could detect DeferBad with an anomaly score of 3.43 (compared to 1.49 for benign models), this was significantly lower than the score of 6.62 for conventional BadNet attacks (Figure \ref{fig:rcs ).}
elements.sent-2411-309: This suggests that while DeferBad is detectable by RCS, it demonstrates improved stealthiness compared to conventional attacks.
elements.sent-2411-310: Furthermore, this relative improvement indicates potential for future refinements of DeferBad to completely evade RCS detection.
elements.sent-2411-311: Scale-Up Detection: Scale-Up \citep{scaleup detects backdoors by examining prediction consistency under image amplification.}
elements.sent-2411-312: DeferBad successfully evaded this detection method, achieving an SPC score of 0.2906, which is slightly lower than benign models (0.3072) and significantly different from BadNet attacks (1.0), as shown in Figure \ref{fig:scaleup .}
elements.sent-2411-313: IDB-PSC Detection: IDB-PSC \citep{idbpsc detects backdoors by analyzing consistency under batch normalization parameter scaling.}
elements.sent-2411-314: Our experiments demonstrated that DeferBad effectively evaded this detection method, with a score of 0.1048 compared to 0.1187 for benign models and 1.0 for BadNet attacks (Figure \ref{fig:idbpsc ).}
elements.sent-2411-315: These additional experiments further validate the stealthiness of DeferBad across a broader range of detection methods, particularly showing strong evasion capabilities against Scale-Up and IDB-PSC detection methods.
